spec_match.rationale: The simulator now compares the staged 255, 256, 257, 512, and 1024 TCP pool sizes and reports latency and queue-wait effects for each.

interactive.rationale: Burst size, request work, workload mix, and rerun controls all drive live queue, latency, and saved-time metrics after the escaped script was repaired.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The page matches the feature framing around changing the per-profile socket pool and observing both performance and side-channel implications.

edge_cases.rationale: The lab covers the 255/257 control branches, the 256 baseline, larger 512/1024 pools, uniform, slow, and fat-tail workload mixes, and visible queue-exhaustion behavior.

design_system.rationale: The page uses shared design variables, keeps button background and text color paired, and avoids static-code-card-only output.

summary: Pool Exhaustion Simulator now runs valid JavaScript, links the correct ChromeStatus feature, and models all staged pool-size branches with visible queue and tail-latency output.
